query: effectiveness of laryngeal mask supreme with simple visual guides for laypersons

retrieved_docs.title: Laryngeal Mask Airway -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Outline the technique for laryngeal mask airway use. Describe the indications for laryngeal mask airways. Review the complications of laryngeal mask airways. Explain the importance of well-coordinated teamwork and clear communication to facilitate the appropriate use of laryngeal mask airways in patients with difficult airways. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Airway Management -- Equipment -- Bougie
retrieved_docs.content: A bougie is a long, semirigid plastic device, also known as a plastic stylet, that is considered an adjunct or rescue device in difficult intubations or part of the primary intubation protocols. This tool serves as a guide to facilitate ETT placement, particularly in scenarios where visualization of the vocal cords is challenging.

retrieved_docs.title: Endotracheal Intubation Techniques -- Equipment -- Back up
retrieved_docs.content: Laryngeal mask airway (LMA) Bougie Cricothyrotomy tray Magill forceps

retrieved_docs.title: Direct Laryngoscopy -- Equipment
retrieved_docs.content: Equipment for direct laryngoscopy also includes adjunct airway management devices such as a "Bougie," which is an ETT introducer, oral and nasal airways, and rescue airway devices such as a Combitube or supraglottic airway tubes. An end-tidal carbon dioxide monitor (capnography) is required to help confirm the appropriate placement of the ETT. Lastly, all direct laryngoscopy equipment includes back-up devices to access the airway, such as video laryngoscopes [13] , rescue airway devices (laryngeal mask airway) and if nothing works, a cricothyrotomy or tracheostomy tray. If a difficult airway is known or suspected, the instruments for a surgical airway should be opened and ready before beginning laryngoscopy.

retrieved_docs.title: Difficult Airway -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: After a difficult airway is identified, one needs to determine what equipment is available to make informed decisions about how to proceed. Preferably, this is done before there is an immediate threat of desaturation. For each practitioner, the difficult airway tools used will be those which they are most familiar with and have achieved the best results with anecdotally. The options include the intubating laryngeal mask airway, fiberoptic bronchoscope, intubating stylet, articulating laryngoscope, video laryngoscope, and cricothyroidotomy.

retrieved_docs.title: Nasopharyngeal Airway -- Equipment
retrieved_docs.content: The following must be available during NPA placement: NPA devices of different sizes Lidocaine jelly Topical vasoconstrictor nasal spray, such as oxymetazoline 0.05% and phenylephrine 0.5% Aerosolized 2% to 4% lidocaine Suction tubing Suction yanker BVM Nasal cannula for apneic oxygenation if patient sedation is considered Alternative airway management equipment, such as a laryngeal mask airway (see Image . Laryngeal Mask Airway), glideslope, bougie, and surgical airway tools

retrieved_docs.title: Direct Laryngoscopy -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: In the cases of very anterior airways, mild-to-moderate cricoid pressure can be applied by an assistant while lifting the laryngoscope to help visualize the airway.

retrieved_docs.title: Laryngeal Mask Airway -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: Prior to the insertion of an LMA, the patient should ideally be fasted and assessed for markers of difficulty or contraindications. This may not be possible in emergency situations where it is used as a rescue device. The LMA comes in various sizes, and recommendations are generally weight-based and printed on the package and the devices. For adults, the following is a simple size guide: size three for adults 30 to 50 kg, size four for adults 50 to 70 kg, and size five for adults over 70 kg. In general, the larger size should be chosen for patients between sizes because it will seal more effectively. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Laryngeal Mask Airway -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Endotracheal intubation can be achieved by inserting the endotracheal tube (ETT) through most second-generation LMAs. Those devices do not have aperture bars at the cuff end and usually have higher seal pressures than the original LMAs that are still frequently used. The LMA Fastrach has a higher rate of intubation success compared to the standard 1 generation and is designed for blind intubation using the company’s proprietary endotracheal tube. However, failure rates can be as high as 40%, and endotracheal intubation is best facilitated under direct visualization using a flexible bronchoscope. Regular endotracheal tubes are usually not long enough to go past the vocal cords when inserted through an LMA. Using either a tube exchanger that slides over a flexible bronchoscope or using a nasal RAE tube, which is longer, will overcome that problem.

retrieved_docs.title: Laryngeal Mask Airway -- Equipment
retrieved_docs.content: The LMA Fastrach, also called the ILMA, is specifically designed to facilitate blind intubation. It is available in both reusable and disposable forms and has a handle designed to allow for optimal positioning and a bar designed to elevate the epiglottis out of the way to facilitate intubation. This device is designed for use with specialized endotracheal proprietary tubes made and packaged with the ILMA.

retrieved_docs.title: Laryngeal Mask Airway -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Success rates for blind intubation through an LMA range from 60 to 99%, depending on the device and skill of the operator. Blind intubation rates are highest for the Fastrach LMA (ILMA) as compared to other supraglottic devices but should best be facilitated by flexible fiberoptic guidance whenever possible.

retrieved_docs.title: Laryngeal Mask Airway -- Personnel
retrieved_docs.content: While initially used by anesthesia providers, success in placing LMAs has been demonstrated by novice intubators, EMS providers, nurses, advanced practice providers, and physicians of multiple specialties.

retrieved_docs.title: EMS Field Intubation -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Following this approach can mitigate potential problems and ensure a provider can respond to challenging situations if necessary. Proper preparation can also increase the likelihood of first-pass success.  First-pass success is critical to help prevent many of the complications described below.  Multiple attempts have been associated with an increased risk of intubation failure and complications. [9] When using direct visualization technique or video laryngoscopy, there is no substitute for seeing the tube pass through the vocal cords to ensure proper placement.  A provider must be well versed in the relevant anatomy of the larynx and posterior pharynx to obtain an adequate view of the patient’s airway. The exact technique varies with the laryngoscope blade choice, but a provider must control the tongue and epiglottis to visualize the vocal cords.  The tongue is controlled by holding the laryngoscope in the provider’s left hand and sweeping it to the patient’s left. The epiglottis is controlled using the tip of the laryngoscope blade, directly when using a Miller blade, or by inserting the blade tip into the vallecula when using a MAC blade, to expose the vocal cords. Once the cords are visualized, the ET tube is inserted with the provider’s right hand until the balloon portion of the tube has just passed the vocal cords.  The stylet is removed, the balloon inflated, and ventilation is provided using a resuscitation bag and reservoir. Placement is then confirmed with ETCO2 colorimetric capnometry or monitored capnography, observing equal chest rise, and auscultation of all lung fields.  Special care is taken to never “rock” the laryngoscope blade in an attempt to obtain a better view. The oropharynx and epiglottis structures are to be lifted in an anterior and caudal direction to avoid damage to the teeth. Excessive force should be avoided, as this could cause damage to the soft tissues as well.  There will be times that direct laryngoscopy, or the use of the video laryngoscopy, will fail on the first attempt at intubation. A provider may have difficulty identifying the necessary anatomical landmarks, need to change equipment, or misplace the ET.  It is customary to allow repeated attempts, but it is generally considered a failed airway after three unsuccessful attempts (Wang HA). [10] Providers should follow the guidance of their local protocols to define a failed airway, the use of airway adjuncts, and the use of rescue devices. It is recommended that providers be skilled in BVM ventilation as well as with the use of rescue supraglottic devices and surgical airways in the case of a failed airway. [2] The most effective deterrents to a failed airway are proper training, experience, and practice. [11] Numerous recommendations have been made for the best way to acquire and maintain competency with airway procedures, but there is no substitute for experience. The National Association of EMS Physicians (NAEMSP) provides consensus recommendations on the “ideal preparation for prehospital RSI” through its position statement and resource documentation on Drug-Assisted Intubation in the Prehospital Setting. [2] Preparation to become efficient in field intubation should incorporate formal didactic training, acquisition of baseline SAI/RSI skills with clinical and operating room experience, and continuing maintenance of SAI/RSI skills. These recommendations do not set a specific number of procedures to gain and maintain competency but highlight the importance of continued education and practice.  Multiple studies have tried to identify the minimum experience required to become proficient with intubation and the necessary rate of use with the procedure to maintain competency. Anesthesia literature has reported that healthcare providers require 50 endotracheal intubations to reach a success rate of 90%. [12] Wang et al. found that a prehospital provider requires 4 to 12 endotracheal intubations a year to maintain competency. [13] Such goals are difficult to reach for most prehospital providers because of a lack of quality operating room (OR) training time and exposure to field intubation in the clinical setting. Many providers only have the opportunity to perform a field intubation one time or less per year. [13] Despite the difficulty reaching the cited thresholds for training and procedure exposure, field intubation rates are improving to more satisfactory levels. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: EMS Confined Space Care -- Issues of Concern -- Airway Management
retrieved_docs.content: Intubating in a confined space has unique challenges. Inline c-spine stabilization may be difficult or impossible. Spacial constraints can prevent intubation from the standard position from behind the patient. This situation often requires the face-to-face technique known as inverse intubation or icepick intubation. The laryngoscope is still held with the right hand and the handle toward the patient’s feet; the incubator may be on the patient's side or straddle the patient. In this position and approach, video laryngoscopy has no clear, proven advantage over traditional laryngoscopy blades. Another technique that may be useful in the unconscious patient is digital intubation. The rescuer uses his or her fingers to lift the epiglottis and then guide the tube through the glottis and into the trachea. When a cricothyrotomy is required, a recent study showed that the Quicktrak proved faster to insert than the Melker kit. The time saving from using the Quicktrack was believed to be secondary to fewer parts and less manipulation required.

retrieved_docs.title: Difficult Airway -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: In an emergency situation, the patient should have the buccal cavity suctioned completely, and bag-mask ventilation should be attempted. If bag-mask ventilation is successful, direct laryngoscopy can be performed. If direct laryngoscopy fails because the vocal cords cannot be visualized due to an anterior larynx, an articulating laryngoscope blade may provide a better view. This blade has a lever attached to the tip which allows the tip a greater range of anterior motion thus elevating the epiglottis and allowing visualization of the vocal cords. If direct laryngoscopy fails, a lighted scope may make the visualization of the cords easier. The endotracheal tube can then be placed under direct video guidance using the specialized, rigid stylet that is placed inside the endotracheal tube. If a lighted scope is not available, placement of an intubating LMA may be done. This device is placed in the posterior pharynx, and the patient can be ventilated through the device. It should be noted that LMAs do not provide a protected airway, and the patient is at risk of aspiration. The iLMA is designed to accept a specific endotracheal tube that should be kept in the same package which passes through the LMA and may blindly enter the trachea based on the appropriate positioning of the tip of the device in the esophagus. The Combitube functions similarly to this in that it is placed blindly. The tip is placed into the esophagus, and the distal balloon inflated, sealing the esophagus. A proximal balloon is then inflated, and the result is the lumen of the ventilatory circuit isolated around the larynx. Positive pressure can then be applied to ventilate the patient. This device also is easily displaced, and other difficulties with cuff pressures can disrupt the mucosal membranes in which they come into contact. A Combitube should not be considered a long-term solution; it does not protect from aspiration.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2146
retrieved_docs.content: The larynx serves as an indirect procedure site for other non‑or therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system, indicating that certain respiratory interventions that are not directly therapeutic to the larynx can nonetheless involve the larynx indirectly. This relationship highlights the importance of recognizing the larynx’s involvement when planning and performing non‑therapeutic respiratory procedures, as indirect effects on the larynx may impact airway function and patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 84
retrieved_docs.content: transglottic tracheal catheterization—all employ the intubation method and are associated with complications such as corneal injury and the condition vascular edema (SMQ); the method also includes placement of a laryngeal mask airway tube. Laser interstitial thermal therapy (LITT) performed under image guidance is placed within Other miscellaneous procedures and, in a gastrointestinal context, under other gastrointestinal therapeutic procedures, highlighting its role as a minimally invasive ablative therapy. Feeding by injection (تغذية بالحقن) is a subset of other non‑operative procedures and is positioned within prophylactic vaccinations and inoculations, linking procedural care with preventive immunization strategies. Nasogastric tube procedures are linked to the nasogastric tube device and map to the clinical activity of nasogastric tube insertion, which serves as a parent concept for non‑incisional irrigation, cleaning, and local instillation of the digestive system, including

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1907
retrieved_docs.content: The larynx serves as an indirect procedure site for other non‑or therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system. The specific intervention “Injection of larynx” includes as a subordinate step the procedure “تركيب مسلك هوائي فموي بلعومي” (placement of an oral‑pharyngeal airway). Likewise, the broader category “other non‑or therapeutic procedures on respiratory system” also lists “تركيب مسلك هوائي فموي بلعومي” as a child procedure. This demonstrates that placement of an oral‑pharyngeal airway is a common subprocedure both within targeted laryngeal injections and within general non‑therapeutic respiratory interventions, underscoring its relevance to airway management across multiple procedural contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 508
retrieved_docs.content: Respiratory intubation and mechanical ventilation is categorized as a placement of an oral and pharyngeal airway (تركيب مسلك هوائي فموي بلعومي). Diagnostic procedures on larynx and trachea are directly related to the larynx, and the larynx serves as an indirect procedure site for other non‑therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system. Injection of larynx includes, as a subordinate concept, the placement of an oral and pharyngeal airway, and the broader group of other non‑therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system also contains this airway‑placement procedure. Together, these connections highlight how airway management interventions such as intubation, mechanical ventilation, and oral‑pharyngeal airway placement are organized within the taxonomy of respiratory procedures, with the larynx functioning as a central anatomical focus for both diagnostic and non‑therapeutic interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2333
retrieved_docs.content: Injection of the larynx includes the subsequent step of تركيب مسلك هوائي فموي بلعومي (placement of an oral‑pharyngeal airway), indicating that airway management is an integral component of the laryngeal injection procedure. This relationship underscores that when clinicians perform a laryngeal injection, they also consider establishing an oral‑pharyngeal airway to maintain airway patency and support effective ventilation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2208
retrieved_docs.content: Injection of larynx is a medical procedure that includes, as a subordinate step, the placement of an oral‑pharyngeal airway (تركيب مسلك هوائي فموي بلعومي). The placement of an oral‑pharyngeal airway is classified among other non‑operating‑room therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system. Consequently, the laryngeal injection is linked to a specific airway management technique that falls within the broader category of non‑surgical respiratory interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2332
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on larynx and trachea are directly associated with the larynx, indicating that the larynx is the primary anatomical focus of these examinations. In addition, the larynx serves as an indirect procedure site for other non‑OR therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system, meaning that it can be involved secondarily in various non‑operative respiratory treatments. Understanding that the larynx functions both as the main target for diagnostic evaluation of the larynx and trachea and as an indirect site for additional respiratory therapies is essential for comprehensive patient assessment and coordinated care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 1470
retrieved_docs.content: The larynx serves as an indirect procedural site for other non‑or therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system. The specific intervention “Injection of larynx” includes as a subordinate procedure “تركيب مسلك هوائي فموي بلعومي” (placement of an oral/pharyngeal airway). Likewise, the broader category “other non‑or therapeutic procedures on respiratory system” lists “تركيب مسلك هوائي فموي بلعومي” as one of its child procedures. In addition, “Respiratory intubation and mechanical ventilation” is classified as “تركيب مسلك هوائي فموي بلعومي,” indicating that intubation and mechanical ventilation are considered forms of oral/pharyngeal airway placement. Together, these connections show that airway placement procedures are integral components of various non‑therapeutic respiratory interventions and are linked to the larynx as an indirect site of action.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1313
retrieved_docs.content: The larynx serves as an indirect site for various other non‑or therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system. Within this broader category, the specific intervention described as تركيب مسلك هوائي فموي بلعومي (placement of an oral‑pharyngeal airway) is classified as a child procedure. Recognizing that the larynx may be indirectly involved in such non‑therapeutic respiratory interventions, including the insertion of an oral‑pharyngeal airway, is important for clinicians when assessing airway management and potential procedural complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1691
retrieved_docs.content: Respiratory intubation and mechanical ventilation is recognized as both the specific medical procedure and the category that defines it, effectively classifying itself within the domain of airway management and ventilatory support.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2148
retrieved_docs.content: Respiratory intubation and mechanical ventilation is classified as تركيب مسلك هوائي فموي بلعومي, indicating that this procedure involves the insertion of an oral and pharyngeal airway to establish and maintain a secure airway for assisted ventilation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 789
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on larynx and trachea are situated within the broader category of other diagnostic procedures of respiratory tract and mediastinum, indicating that examinations of the larynx and trachea are considered part of the general set of respiratory‑tract diagnostic activities. Therapeutic interventions such as Injection of larynx and Injection of locally‑acting therapeutic substance into trachea are each classified as throat surgery, reflecting that these injections are surgical procedures performed on the upper airway. Although throat surgery is primarily a therapeutic classification, it is linked to the larger diagnostic group of other diagnostic procedures of respiratory tract and mediastinum, highlighting that certain interventions on the larynx and trachea can serve both diagnostic and therapeutic roles. Recognizing this overlap helps clinicians select appropriate procedures and coordinate care for patients requiring evaluation or treatment of laryngeal and tracheal conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1263
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on larynx and trachea are directly linked to the larynx, indicating that examinations and assessments targeting these structures focus on the laryngeal anatomy. The relationship identified as “indirect_procedure_site_of” connects the larynx to the broader category “other non-or therapeutic procedures on respiratory system,” meaning that the larynx can serve as an indirect site for non‑operative therapeutic interventions within the respiratory system. Within this category, the relationship “has child” designates the specific procedure “تركيب مسلك هوائي فموي بلعومي” (placement of an oral and pharyngeal airway) as a subordinate or child procedure. Consequently, the placement of an oral/pharyngeal airway is classified under “other non-or therapeutic procedures on respiratory system” and may involve the larynx indirectly. Recognizing these interconnected relationships aids clinicians in coordinating diagnostic evaluations of the larynx with therapeutic airway management strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 4 -- Community 2697
retrieved_docs.content: Injection of larynx is linked to the procedure described by the Arabic term “تركيب مسلك هوائي فموي بلعومي,” indicating that laryngeal injection involves or leads to the placement of an oral‑pharyngeal airway. Similarly, respiratory intubation and mechanical ventilation is classified under the same Arabic‑language category “تركيب مسلك هوائي فموي بلعومي,” showing that endotracheal intubation with assisted ventilation is considered a form of oral‑pharyngeal airway insertion. Together, these relationships illustrate that both laryngeal injection and respiratory intubation with mechanical ventilation are integral components of airway management techniques that fall within the broader concept of oral and pharyngeal airway placement.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 1224
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on larynx and trachea are directly related to the larynx and are organized as a subcategory of other diagnostic procedures of respiratory tract and mediastinum. According to the indirect_procedure_site_of relationship, the larynx also serves as an indirect procedure site for other non‑or therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system. This dual classification highlights the larynx’s central role both as the primary anatomical focus of specific diagnostic interventions and as a secondary site affected by broader non‑therapeutic respiratory procedures, emphasizing its importance in the overall assessment and management of respiratory tract conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 1198
retrieved_docs.content: Nasogastric tube procedures classify the nasogastric tube, indicating that the range of clinical actions, techniques, and protocols are organized around the use, insertion, management, and care of the nasogastric tube.
